problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem A (continuous): build a model that, given a cyclist's power profile, the course terrain (turns, hills), and weather, prescribes the optimal power-output distribution along an individual time trial (ITT) course so as to minimize finish time; define power profiles for at least a time-trial specialist and a sprinter across genders; apply to real courses; do sensitivity analysis on power deviation and weather; and extend to team time trials (TTT). This O-award paper ("How to Ride a Cycling Time Trial", team 2212245) solves it by building a first-principles physiological energy-supply model bottom-up, coupling it to a Newtonian bicycle-motion model, and deriving terrain-specific "maintain threshold speed" optimal strategies.
models_used: Three-compartment energy-supply ODE model: ATP-CP (phosphagen) system, glycolysis system, and aerobic system, each tracked as a substance-content ODE (V_CP, V_O2, V_LA) with conversion efficiencies alpha_i, physiological max ATP-production-rate caps, and restoration/recovery dynamics; total power P=P1+P2+P3 via P=mu*v_ATP
min{} saturation operator on each compartment's ATP rate to encode physiological rate ceilings (e.g. P1 = mu*min{alpha1*V_CP/tau, v_ATP1_max})
Over-threshold (anaerobic) extension: over-limit constant e>1 allowing P<=e(P1+P2+P3) at the cost of extra lactate dV_LA = b[P-(P1+P2+P3)]dt to model an adrenaline/all-out final effort
Greedy dominant-system selection algorithm (flowchart, Fig. 4): recruit aerobic, then glycolytic, then ATP-CP systems in order until demanded power P is met or declared unachievable

Power-profile reconstruction: summing the three compartment power curves to produce a full power-duration curve, validated against the four canonical cycling benchmark powers (5 s sprint, 1 min anaerobic, 5 min VO2max, 1 h FTP)
Newtonian bicycle-cyclist motion model with work-energy theorem: effective mass m* = m + 2I/R^2 (includes wheel rotational inertia), m*a = F - f_a - f_r - f_s, with eta*P = F*v drivetrain coupling

Resistance sub-models: aerodynamic drag f_a = (1/2)C*rho*S*v_r^2 simplified to p*v_r^2; rolling resistance f_r = C_rr*m*g*cos(alpha); slope/gravity resistance f_s = m*g*sin(alpha); drivetrain efficiency eta=0.95
Cornering model (inclination/lean method): max corner speed v_m = sqrt(g*r*k) from friction-limited centripetal force, k~0.3

Optimal-strategy derivation: proves constant 'threshold speed' (where threshold power exactly sustains constant v) beats accelerate/decelerate alternation, via energy comparison Pt = a*integral(v^3 dt) + f*integral(v dt) (convexity of the v^3 aero term penalizes speed variation)
Course reconstruction model: real Tokyo 2020 and Belgium 2021 UCI courses rebuilt from official roadmaps as straights + arcs + estimated elevation profile (length error 3-4.5%)

Team Time Trial extension: drafting reduces follower aero drag to ~75% (mu*P1 = v(0.75*f_air+f_other)); rotating pace-line pull model with constraint T1 <= 5*T0 (recovery vs pull time for a 6-rider line); separate optimized final-sprint break-away model maximizing L/v0 - sum(t_i)
key_techniques: Bottom-up mechanistic modeling: instead of curve-fitting an empirical power-duration curve (the common Critical Power / W' approach), they DERIVE the power curve from underlying biochemistry, so the same parameters explain sprint vs endurance differences and recovery
Using saturation (min{}) operators to fuse capacity limits with rate limits cleanly inside ODEs

Parameter-as-archetype design: a single parameter table (M_CP, v_in, M_O2, M_LA, three v_ATP_max) instantiates each rider archetype (TT specialist vs sprinter, male vs female), so all downstream results are reproducible by swapping ~7 numbers
Weight normalization (W/kg) to make power values rider-mass-independent and directly comparable to published benchmark data
Validation-by-benchmark: comparing modeled 5s/1min/5min/FTP against real-world published values to argue model reliability before using it
Closing the loop with reality: simulating finish times on real championship courses and comparing to the actual champion's time as an external sanity check
Integral averaging of a time-varying forcing term: deriving average wind resistance over a turn by integrating v_r^2 over the cornering arc to get closed-form mean drag for 90deg and 180deg turns
Decomposing a hard optimization into terrain-typed cases (flat straight / curve / uphill / downhill) and stitching per-segment optimal sub-strategies into a global strategy via a decision flowchart
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective the decisive differentiator is the energy-supply model: most teams treat the rider's power curve as a given black-box input, but this team built it from physiology (ATP-CP / glycolysis / aerobic ODEs) and then re-derived the four canonical benchmark powers, matching published data. That single move makes everything downstream feel principled rather than fitted, gives a clean mechanism to distinguish sprinter vs specialist and male vs female (just change parameters), and crucially provides a recovery model so the strategy can reason about 'rest now, sprint the hill later' tradeoffs that a static power curve cannot. It is mechanistically deep yet still closes with reality (53.0 vs 57.8 min on the real Belgium course). The paper is also complete against every prompt requirement (both archetypes x both genders, three courses including a self-designed stress-test course, power-deviation AND weather sensitivity, and a genuinely modeled TTT with drafting + pace-line + sprint break-away). The robustness result that a 30% power deviation costs only ~3% time is exactly the kind of reassuring, decision-useful headline judges reward. The standalone one-page 'Rider's Race Guidance' for the Directeur Sportif directly answers the deliverable and shows audience awareness."
section_highlights: "Structure is modular and reads in the intended causal order: energy model -> power profiles (application/validation of energy model) -> motion model -> per-terrain optimal strategy -> real-course application -> sensitivity -> TTT extension. The Summary explicitly previews this chain and even flags that 'power curves are an intermediate result' (signaling the team understands what is fundamental vs derived). Strong concrete touches: an epigraph quoting mathematician-Olympian Anna Kiesenhofer to motivate the math-helps-cycling framing; clear notation table up front; flowcharts for the energy-recruitment algorithm and the strategy-selection decision tree; logarithmic-axis power plots to expose the short-duration behavior; per-course velocity/power-vs-position plots showing the strategy responding to terrain; and a polished, plain-language, jargon-light 'Rider's Race Guidance' memo aimed squarely at the Directeur Sportif. Quantified headline results are surfaced repeatedly (53.02 min vs champion 57.78; 30% deviation -> 3.11% time; TTT +14.43% cruise / +18.52% sprint), which is good judge-facing communication."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The headline validation is actually a red flag if scrutinized: the model predicts a FASTER time (53.02 min) than the real champion (57.78 min) on Belgium, yet the text calls results 'slightly inferior to the actual' - the model is optimistic/under-resisted and the self-assessment misreads its own numbers

The energy-supply ODE parameters (M_CP, v_ATP_max, alpha_i, k, b, e, mu) are largely hand-tuned to reproduce benchmark powers; there is no calibration procedure, uncertainty, or identifiability discussion, so the 'mechanistic' rigor is partly cosmetic

Optimality is asserted, not proven: they show 'maintain threshold speed' beats one alternative (symmetric accelerate/decelerate) but never establish global optimality - they themselves admit a variational/optimal-control formulation was the right tool but was skipped for time

Several strong simplifying assumptions: cyclists as point masses, all riders of a gender share one average weight, constant-rate biochemistry, turns assumed flat and taken at uniform speed, and TTT assumes 6 identical riders with idealized instantaneous hand-offs

Crude course reconstruction (elevation 'assigned by estimation', curves with r>60 m ignored) and a stated 13 km/h average-speed figure that is implausibly low for an elite ITT, hinting at unit/data sloppiness

Sensitivity analysis is narrow: power-deviation sensitivity uses only the self-designed course, and weather analysis covers only wind direction/speed plus a hand-wavy rain-via-friction argument; no sensitivity on the many physiological parameters
Some OCR/exposition artifacts and inconsistent equation labeling (e.g. Fig. 20 mislabeled, a stray mu*P appearing where eta*P is meant) reduce polish in the back half
reusable_patterns: Derive-don't-assume the central input: when a problem hands you an empirical curve (here a power-duration curve), build a mechanistic generative model for it and validate against known benchmark points - this is the move that most impressed judges and is transferable to any 'capacity/performance curve' problem
Archetype-by-parameter-table: encode each entity class (sprinter/specialist, male/female) as one row of a parameter table feeding the same model, making results reproducible and comparisons fair

Layer models in a causal stack: physiological/supply model -> mechanical/motion model -> decision/strategy model, each consuming the previous layer's output, so the strategy is grounded in physics that is grounded in physiology
Saturation (min{capacity-rate, max-rate}) operators to express dual constraints inside ODE-based supply models

Convexity argument for steady-state optimality: when cost scales like integral(v^3), use Jensen/convexity to argue constant pace beats variable pace - a clean, reusable proof sketch for pacing/throughput problems

Terrain/regime decomposition + decision flowchart: split a continuous control problem into a small set of regimes (flat/curve/uphill/downhill), solve each, and stitch via an explicit if-then decision tree that doubles as a figure
Validate by replaying reality: rebuild the actual competition course from public maps and compare simulated finish time to the real champion's time as an external check
Ship a stakeholder-facing deliverable: a separate plain-language memo (the 'Rider's Race Guidance') that translates model results into actionable advice for the named audience (Directeur Sportif)
Normalize out a nuisance variable (divide by rider weight -> W/kg) so model output is directly comparable to external reference data
Flag the better method you didn't have time for (variational/optimal control) as honest future work - shows mathematical maturity without requiring the full implementation
